title: How the Long-Lost Body of Richard III Was Found Under a Parking Lot: Solving a 500-Year-Old Mystery
description:
in History |   	March 12th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

ShakeбґspeareБ─≥s The Tragedy of Richard the Third begins with the eponyбґmous charбґacбґter utterбґ
body:
in History |   	March 12th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

ShakeбґspeareБ─≥s The Tragedy of Richard the Third begins with the eponyбґmous charбґacбґter utterбґing the famous line Б─°Now is the winбґter of our disбґconбґtent.Б─² It ends at the Batбґtle of Bosworth Field, by which point his vilбґlainбґous schemes have come to ruin and his deserбґtion by Lord Stanбґley seems to have sealed his fate. Б─°A horse, a horse, my kingбґdom for a horse,Б─² he cries out, coinбґing anothбґer expresбґsion used four cenбґturies latбґer before being slain by the Earl of Richбґmond, the man who would be Henбґry VII. Though Shakeбґspeare himбґself was writбґing more than 100 years after the hisбґtorбґiбґcal events he draбґmaбґtized, he includбґed litбґtle after the event of RichardБ─≥s death, whose most fasбґciбґnatбґing mysбґtery was in any case only solved in our own time.
You can see the stoбґry of Richard IIIБ─≥s long-unknown whereбґabouts in the Priбґmal Space video above. Accordбґing to records, says the narбґraбґtor, Б─°he was buried uncerбґeбґmoбґniбґousбґly beneath the Greyfriбґars Church in Leicesбґter, and a monбґuбґment was evenбґtuбґalбґly placed above his grave.Б─² When Henбґry VIII ordered such housбґes of worбґship shut down forty years latбґer, Greyfriбґars was among the instiбґtuбґtions demolбґished.
Everyбґone evenбґtuбґalбґly came to believe that, amid this destrucбґtion, RichardБ─≥s body had been exhumed and tossed off the Bow Bridge. Only in the earбґly two-thouбґsands did a search for his corpse comбґmence in earnest, spearбґheadбґed by the Richard III Sociбґety. Havбґing deterбґmined that the Bow Bridge stoбґry had been made up, the sociбґetyБ─≥s memбґbers then had to pin down the long-conбґfused forбґmer locaбґtion of Greyfriбґars Church.
One of them, Philipбґpa Lanбґgбґley, got the hunch to start lookбґing under a Leicesбґter parkбґing lot. Budбґgetary limбґiбґtaбґtions forced her team to try digбґging just three trenchбґes across spaces likeбґliбґest to cross the churchБ─≥s footбґprint. Б─°Amazбґingбґly, just six hours into the first day, they came across a skeleбґtonБ─² with skull damбґage and spinal curбґvaбґture. Richard was indeed described as a Б─°hunchбґbackБ─² in his lifeбґtime, but in the twenбґty-first cenбґtuбґry, only DNA eviбґdence closбґes the case. Its acquiбґsiбґtion necesбґsiбґtatбґed both findбґing a couбґple unbroбґken female lines (the only means of transбґmitбґting mitoбґchonбґdrбґiбґal DNA) from his sisбґter down to livбґing, testable indiбґvidбґuбґals while carбґbon-datбґing the skeleбґton. Sure enough, Richard turned out to have been in eterбґnal repose not just under that parkбґing lot, but near a stenбґciled letбґter R Б─■ the kind of coinбґciбґdence from which even the Bard himбґself might have shied away.
Relatбґed conбґtent:
Conбґfirmed: The Bones of Richard III (1452Б─⌠1485) Found Under a UK Parkбґing Lot
74 Ways Charбґacбґters Die in ShakespeareБ─≥s Plays Shown in a Handy Infoбґgraphбґic: From Snakebites to Lack of Sleep
How Engбґland First Became Engбґland: An Aniбґmatбґed Hisбґtoбґry
Hear What Hamбґlet, Richard III & King Lear Soundбґed Like in ShakespeareБ─≥s Origбґiбґnal Proбґnunбґciбґaбґtion
A New Analyбґsis of BeethovenБ─≥s DNA Reveals That Lead Poiбґsonбґing Could Have Caused His Deafбґness
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: The Monty Python Philosophy Soccer Match: The Ancient Greeks Versus the Germans
description:
in Comedy, Sports |   	March 13th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Today, weБ─≥re revisбґitбґing a clasбґsic Monбґty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich
body:
in Comedy, Sports |   	March 13th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Today, weБ─≥re revisбґitбґing a clasбґsic Monбґty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich Olympics. The event is a football/soccer match, pitбґting Gerбґman philosoбґphers against Greek philosoбґphers. On the one side, the Gerбґmans Б─■ Hegel, Nietбґzsche, Kant, Marx and, um, Franz Beckбґenбґbauer. On the othбґer side, Archimedes, Socrates, Plaбґto and the rest of the gang. The refбґerбґee? Conбґfuбґcius. Of course.
Enjoy!

title: Hear Seven Hours of Women Making Electronic Music (1938Б─⌠2014)
description:
Image via Wikiбґmeбґdia Comбґmons
A numбґber of years ago, in a post on the pioбґneerбґing comбґposбґer of the origбґiбґnal Docбґtor Who theme, we wrote tha
body:
Image via Wikiбґmeбґdia Comбґmons
A numбґber of years ago, in a post on the pioбґneerбґing comбґposбґer of the origбґiбґnal Docбґtor Who theme, we wrote that Б─°the earбґly era of experбґiбґmenбґtal elecбґtronбґic music belonged to Delia Derбґbyshire.Б─² DerbyshireБ─■who almost gave Paul McCartбґney a verбґsion of Б─°YesбґterбґdayБ─² with an elecбґtronбґic backбґing in place of stringsБ─■helped invent the earбґly elecбґtronбґic music of the sixбґties through her work with the Radioбґphonбґic Workбґshop, the sound effects labбґoбґraбґtoбґry of the BBC. She went on to form one of the most influбґenбґtial, if largeбґly obscure, elecбґtronбґic acts of the decade, White Noise. And yet, callбґing the earбґly eras of elecбґtronбґic music hers is an exagбґgerбґaбґtion. Of course her many colбґlabбґoбґraбґtors deserve menбґtion, as well as musiбґcians like Bruce Haack, Pierre Henбґry, Kraftwerk, Briбґan Eno, and so many othбґers. But what gets almost comбґpleteбґly left out of many hisбґtoбґries of elecбґtronбґic music, as with so many othбґer hisбґtoбґries, is the promiбґnent role so many women besides Derбґbyshire played in the develбґopбґment of the sounds we now hear around us all the time.
In recogбґniбґtion of this fact, musiбґcian, DJ, and Б─°escaped housewife/schoolteacherБ─² Barбґbara Goldбґen devotбґed two episodesб═of her KPFA radio proбґgram Б─°Crack oБ─≥ DawnБ─² to women in elecбґtronбґic music, once in 2010 and again in 2013. She shares each broadбґcast with co-host Jon Leiбґdeckбґer (Б─°WobбґblyБ─²), and in each segбґment, the two banбґter in casuбґal radio show style, offerбґing hisбґtoбґry and conбґtext for each musiбґcian and comбґposбґer. Highбґlightбґed on UbuБ─≥s forбґmer Twitбґter stream, the first show, Б─°Women in Elecбґtronбґic Music 1938Б─⌠1982 Part 1Б─² (above) gives Derбґbyshire her due, with three tracks from her, includбґing the Docбґtor Who theme.
It also includes music from twenбґty oneб═othбґer comбґposers, beginбґning with Clara Rockбґmore, a refinбґer and popбґuбґlarбґizбґer of the theremin, that weird instruбґment designed to simбґuбґlate a high, tremuбґlous human voice. Also feaбґtured is Wendy CarlosБ─≥s Б─°Timesteps,Б─² an origбґiбґnal piece from her A Clockбґwork Orange score.
The secбґond show, above, fills in sevбґerбґal gaps in the origбґiбґnal broadбґcast and Б─°could easбґiбґly be six hoursБ─² says co-host Leiбґdeckбґer, givбґen the sheer amount of elecбґtronбґic music out there comбґposed and recordбґed by women over the past sevбґenбґtyб═years. This show includes one of our host GoldenБ─≥s own comбґpoбґsiбґtions, Б─°Melody Sumбґnбґer Carбґnaбґhan,Б─² as well as music from Lauбґrie Anderбґson and musique conбґcrete comбґposбґer Doris Hays. These two broadбґcasts alone covбґer an enorбґmous range of stylбґisбґtic and techбґnoбґlogбґiбґcal ground, but for even more discoбґgraphбґiбґcal hisбґtoбґry of women in elecбґtronбґic music, see the playlist below, comбґpiled by Б─°NerdбґgirlБ─² Antye Greie-Ripatбґti. Comбґmisбґsioned by Club Transбґmeбґdiбґale Berlin, the mix includes such well-known names as Yoko Ono, Bjork, and M.I.A., as well as foreбґmothбґers Derбґbyshire and Carбґlos, and dozens more.
In lieu of the radio-show chatбґter of Goldбґen and Leiбґdeckбґer, we have Greie-RipattiБ─≥s post detailбґing each artistБ─≥s time periбґod, counбґtry of oriбґgin, and conбґtriбґbuбґtions to elecбґtronбґic music hisбґtoбґry. Many of the comбґposers repбґreбґsentбґed here worked for major radio and film stuбґdios, scored feaбґture films (like 1956Б─≥s Forбґbidбґden Planбґet), inventбґed and innoбґvatбґed new instruбґments and techбґniques, wrote for orchesбґtras, and passed on their knowlбґedge as eduбґcaбґtors and proбґducбґers. Greie-RipattiБ─≥s page quotes a Danбґish elecбґtronбґic proбґducбґer and perбґformer sayбґing Б─°there is a lot of women in elecбґtronбґic musicБ─і invisбґiбґble women.Б─² Thanks to efforts like hers and GoldenБ─≥s, these pioбґneerбґing creбґators need no longer go unseen or, more imporбґtantбґly, unheard.
Note: An earбґliбґer verбґsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
Relatбґed Conбґtent:
Meet Delia Derбґbyshire, theб═Dr. Whoб═Comбґposбґer Who Almost Turned The BeaбґtбґlesБ─≥ Б─°YesбґterбґdayБ─² Into Earбґly Elecбґtronбґiбґca
Watch Comбґposбґer Wendy Carбґlos Demo an Origбґiбґnal Moog Synбґtheбґsizбґer (1989)
Meet Four Women Who Pioбґneered Elecбґtronбґic Music: Daphne Oram, Lauбґrie Spiegel, ц┴liane Radigue & Pauline Olivбґeros
New Docбґuбґmenбґtaryб═Sisбґters with Tranбґsisбґtorsб═Tells the Stoбґry of Elecбґtronбґic MusicБ─≥s Female Pioбґneers
Mr. Rogers Introбґduces Kids to Experбґiбґmenбґtal Elecбґtronбґic Music by Bruce Haack & Esther Nelбґson (1968)
Hearб═Elecбґtronбґic Ladyбґland, a Mixбґtape Feaбґturбґing 55 Tracks from 35 Pioбґneerбґing Women in Elecбґtronбґic Music
Thomas Dolбґby Explains How a Synбґtheбґsizбґer Works on a Jim Henбґson Kids Show (1989)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiбґcian based in Durham, NC.б═

title: A Visit to Tomц║s Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice
description: What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? Tomц║s Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21,
body:
What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? Tomц║s Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21, in which filmmakers visit his Berlin studio and examine the machinations of his collaborative practice, extending from a team of people to the tiny critters beneath our feet.
Saraceno continually considers how humans occupy space and how such environments inform the ways we connect with the world around us. This short documentary, which is part of the Б─°Realms of the RealБ─² episode, reviews several of the artistБ─≥s projects, from his suspended installations to his more participatory community projects.



Several artworks presented in the film have been previously featured on Colossal, and the film offers insight into the evolution of SaracenoБ─≥s thinking over several years. Much of his work strives for connection and empathy building, which he explains through the structure of the web. Б─°ItБ─≥s really trying to extend the ability of understanding who is our family, right? Who is our brothers, sisters, and grandfathers?Б─² he says. Б─°By allowing others to admire these incredible webs, they will become more empathetic.Б─²
Find more Art21 films on YouTube, along with some of our favorites previously on Colossal.

schema.headline: A Visit to Tomц║s Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice

title: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints
description: Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled
body:
Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled Off the Grid emerged from a familiar yet annoying scenario: owning an older device that can no longer be updated, rendering it practically unusable. EndrullatБ─≥s frustration led him to a moment of impulsivity as he pushed his device through a printing press, coincidentally discovering the distinctive technique.
Б─°One of the most interesting aspects of the process is how clearly the progressive destruction of the devices becomes visible after each print,Б─² the artist explains. With each pass through the device becomes increasingly altered, revealing details about its internal structures and their previous ownersБ─■worn keys that must have been used the most, traces of sticker residue, or remnants of webcam covers. Wiping off ink in between prints also becomes more challenging throughout the process, as glass screens and touchpads begin to crack.

The artistБ─≥s studio printing press is just about one hundred years old, introducing a fascinating contrast between the longevity of toolsБ─■both robust and enduring or short-lived, perhaps even reminiscent of planned obsolescence. Б─°The series reflects on materiality, consumption, and the hidden architectures ofdigital devices,Б─² he says. Б─°Although I might also simply enjoy the sound a laptop makes the first time it runs through the press.Б─²
Endrullat hopes to experiment with printing entire computer setups, complete with mice, keyboards, monitors, and more, which would eventually call for a different type of press. Take a peek at the artistБ─≥s process and find more work on his Instagram.

schema.headline: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints

title: In 'Life Forms,' Janny Baek Imagines a Speculative Landscape
description:
Joy Machine is pleased to presentб═Life Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change?
body:
Joy Machine is pleased to presentб═Life Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change? With fear, excitement, or uncertainty? As Janny Baek builds sculptural ceramics of speculative beings and imagined landscapes, she grapples with these questions. The work follows its own dream logic, one that accepts incongruity and dissonance as necessary to play and experimentation. Marbling hunks of colored clay, coiling bases, and molding a singular material into something new is part of an exploratory practice that embraces transformation and its often strange outcomes.
Detail of Б─°Dream StateБ─² (2024)
Life Formsб═emerges from this dual meaning, invoking both the act of creation and the fantastical works it produces. Neither wholly abstract nor representational, BaekБ─≥s sculptures draw on natural structures and processes and invite us to question how we interpret the world around us. Recognizable forms like open blossoms, birds, and creatures are met with the unexpected. These lively components make even the more abstract works appear animate, like ambiguous organisms that might decide to scuttle away. They evoke something primordial and yet are exhilaratingly new.
Baek paints in the way she sketches, as a means of developing ideas and visualizing their potential. For her ceramics, she incorporates hand building alongside the Japanese pottery technique known asб═nerikomi, which involves splicing and designing patterns with strips of colored clay. Б─°My material choices are a way of thinking about natural processes: color gradients as the continuous nature of change, a multitude of colors as potential, abundance, and vitality, and patterns as signals and communications,Б─² she says.
Hovering between worlds, BaekБ─≥s work populates a speculative environment in which beings morph, mutate, and blossom, their individual features forming an otherworldly lineage thatБ─≥s recognizable but not identical. While Б─°temporarily and imperfectly captured in a moment of many possible transformations,Б─² the works beckon us into a world in which change is not only inevitable but also the most alluring proposition.
Life Formsб═is BaekБ─≥s Chicago debut. An opening reception will be held on March 20, and the artist will be present.б═See more from Baek previously on Colossal.
Б─°Future BlossomsБ─² (2023), colored porcelain, 13 x 13 x 12 inches
Б─°LandscapeБ─² (2025), colored stoneware with glazed interior, 15 x 8 x 8 inches
Detail of Б─°Dream StateБ─² (2024)
Б─°Dream State 2Б─² (2025), stoneware, colored slip, porcelain, and glaze, 21 x 17 x 16 inches
Detail of Б─°LandscapeБ─² (2025)
Б─°Plant LifeБ─² (2025), stoneware, colored stoneware, and glaze, 21.5 x 15 x 15 inches
Detail of Б─°Plant LifeБ─² (2025)
